session.user:
Add `curated_in: erk-tui` to the YAML frontmatter of the following docs/learned/tui/ files. The field should be added as the LAST field before the closing `---` in each file's frontmatter block.
For each file: 1. Read the file to see its current frontmatter 2. Find the closing `---` of the frontmatter block 3. Add `curated_in: erk-tui` as a new line just before the closing `---`
Files to edit (all in /home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/tui/): 1. data-contract.md 2. derived-display-columns.md 3. dual-handler-pattern.md 4. filter-pipeline.md 5. filter-toggle-pattern.md 6. frozen-dataclass-field-management.md 7. keyboard-shortcuts.md 8. lifecycle-display.md 9. modal-screen-pattern.md 10. modal-widget-embedding.md
For example, if a file has: ``` --- title: Some Title read_when: - "some condition" tripwires: - action: "something" warning: "something" last_audited: "2026-02-05 00:00 PT" audit_result: edited --- ```
It should become: ``` --- title: Some Title read_when: - "some condition" tripwires: - action: "something" warning: "something" last_audited: "2026-02-05 00:00 PT" audit_result: edited curated_in: erk-tui --- ```
IMPORTANT: The `curated_in: erk-tui` line goes after ALL existing fields, immediately before the closing `---`.
Do NOT modify any content after the frontmatter block.
session.assistant: I'll add `curated_in: erk-tui` to the YAML frontmatter of all 10 files in the docs/learned/tui/ directory. Let me start by reading each file to see their current frontmatter.
session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: TUI Data Contract 3→read_when: 4→ - "building an alternate frontend consuming plan data" 5→ - "adding fields to PlanRowData or PlanDataProvider" 6→ - "understanding the display-vs-raw field duality" 7→ - "serializing plan data to JSON for external consumers" 8→tripwires: 9→ - action: "formatting display strings during table render" 10→ warning: "Display strings are pre-formatted at fetch time. Add new *_display fields to PlanRowData and format in RealPlanDataProvider._build_row_data(), not in the widget layer." 11→ - action: "adding a field to PlanRowData without updating make_plan_row" 12→ warning: "The fake's make_plan_row() helper must stay in sync. Add the new field with a sensible default there too, or all TUI tests will break." 13→ - action: "putting PlanDataProvider ABC in src/erk/tui/" 14→ warning: "The ABC lives in erk-shared so provider implementations are co-located in the shared package. External consumers import from erk-shared alongside other shared gateways." 15→ - action: "constructing PlanFilters without copying all fields from existing filters" 16→ warning: "All fields must be explicitly copied in _load_data() PlanFilters construction. Missing fields (like creator) cause silent filtering failures." 17→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 18→audit_result: edited 19→--- 20→ 21→# TUI Data Contract 22→ 23→The TUI data layer is designed around a single frozen dataclass (`PlanRowData`) that carries both display-ready strings and raw identifiers. This document explains the design decisions behind the data contract, the cross-package split, and the pitfalls of JSON serialization — things that can't be learned by reading any single source file. 24→ 25→## Why Display and Raw Fields Coexist 26→ 27→<!-- Source: src/erk/tui/data/types.py, PlanRowData --> 28→ 29→`PlanRowData` carries two kinds of fields for the same data. For example, `pr_number` (raw `int | None`) and `pr_display` (pre-formatted `str` like `"#123 👀"`). This is deliberate: 30→
... [215 lines omitted] ...
143→| `run_conclusion` | `str` | Yes | Workflow run conclusion: `"success"`, `"failure"`, `"cancelled"`, etc. | 225→Adding a field to `PlanRowData` touches at minimum five places. Missing any one causes test failures or silent data loss:

1→--- 2→title: Derived Display Columns 3→read_when: 4→ - "adding a TUI column that uses an existing PlanRowData field" 5→ - "deciding whether a new column needs gateway/query changes" 6→--- 7→ 8→# Derived Display Columns 9→ 10→When a new TUI column reuses an already-present `PlanRowData` field (no new data needed), you can skip the gateway and data-layer steps from the standard [5-step column addition pattern](column-addition-pattern.md). 11→ 12→## When This Applies 13→ 14→The standard 5-step pattern assumes the column requires new data from the backend. If the data already exists in `PlanRowData` (perhaps fetched for a different purpose), skip steps 1, 2, 4, and 5. Only step 3 (add column + value in table) is needed. 15→ 16→## Reduced Checklist 17→ 18→| Step | File | Change | 19→| ---- | --------------------------- | ----------------------------- | 20→| 3 | `tui/widgets/plan_table.py` | Add column definition + value | 21→ 22→Plus: update all index-based test assertions (see column index cascade tripwire in [column-addition-pattern.md](column-addition-pattern.md)). 23→ 24→## Example: Branch Column 25→ 26→The branch column displays `pr_head_branch` and `worktree_branch` — both already present in `PlanRowData` for other purposes (PR discovery, worktree display). No gateway query, data class field, or serialization changes were needed. Only the column definition and render logic were added to `plan_table.py`. 27→ 28→## Decision: Standard vs Derived 29→ 30→| Question | Answer | Pattern |
... [15 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Dual Provider Pattern for Context-Agnostic Commands 3→read_when: 4→ - "implementing a TUI command that works from both list and detail views" 5→ - "understanding how MainListCommandProvider and PlanCommandProvider share commands" 6→ - "adding command palette support to a new screen" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "duplicating command definitions for list and detail contexts" 9→ warning: "Commands are defined once in the registry. Use a second Provider subclass with its own _get_context() to serve the same commands from a new context." 10→ - action: "duplicating execute_palette_command logic between ErkDashApp and PlanDetailScreen" 11→ warning: "This duplication is a known trade-off. Both ErkDashApp.execute_palette_command() and PlanDetailScreen.execute_command() implement the same command_id switch because they dispatch to different APIs (provider methods vs executor methods). See the asymmetries section below." 12→ - action: "showing toast from a modal screen" 13→ warning: "Call self.dismiss() before app-level toasts. Modal blocks the correct z-layer, so toasts must render at app level after modal dismissal." 14→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 15→audit_result: clean 16→--- 17→ 18→# Dual Provider Pattern for Context-Agnostic Commands 19→ 20→The TUI command palette serves the same set of commands from two different UI contexts (main list and plan detail modal) without duplicating command definitions. This is achieved through Textual's `Provider` mechanism: two provider classes share one command registry but resolve `CommandContext` from different sources. 21→ 22→## Why Two Providers, Not Two Registries 23→ 24→The core design question was: when a user opens the command palette from the detail modal, should it show a different set of commands than from the main list? The answer is no — the same operations (open PR, copy checkout, close plan) apply regardless of context. What differs is only _which plan_ the command operates on and _how the command dispatches_. 25→ 26→<!-- Source: src/erk/tui/commands/provider.py, MainListCommandProvider --> 27→<!-- Source: src/erk/tui/commands/provider.py, PlanCommandProvider --> 28→ 29→`MainListCommandProvider` resolves context from the table's selected row (`_app._get_selected_row()`), while `PlanCommandProvider` resolves context from the detail screen's `_row` field. Both call the same `get_available_commands()` with the resolved `CommandContext`, so command definitions, availability predicates, and display names are defined exactly once. 30→
... [83 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: TUI Filter Pipeline Pattern 3→read_when: 4→ - "adding a new filter to the TUI dashboard" 5→ - "understanding how objective/stack/text filters interact" 6→ - "modifying the escape key behavior in the TUI" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "adding a new filter without updating the escape chain" 9→ warning: "New filter implementations must add an entry to `action_exit_app()` progressive escape chain. Missing entries leave filters stuck with no way for the user to clear them." 10→ - action: "changing filter application order in _apply_filter_and_sort()" 11→ warning: "Filter order is intentional: objective → stack → text → sort. Objective is broadest (cross-stack), stack is mid-level, text is narrowest. Changing order produces unexpected results." 12→ - action: "using a mutable set for _stack_filter_branches" 13→ warning: "Stack filter branches use frozenset[str] for immutability and efficient membership testing. Do not use set or list." 14→--- 15→ 16→# TUI Filter Pipeline Pattern 17→ 18→The TUI dashboard applies filters in a defined pipeline sequence: objective filter, stack filter, text filter, then sort. Each filter follows the same toggle pattern with state variables, toggle actions, and escape chain integration. 19→ 20→## Filter Pipeline 21→ 22→`_apply_filter_and_sort()` in `src/erk/tui/app.py` applies filters in sequence: 23→ 24→1. **Objective filter**: Filters rows matching `_objective_filter_issue` 25→2. **Stack filter**: Filters rows matching branches in `_stack_filter_branches` 26→3. **Text filter**: Applies text search query if `FilterMode.ACTIVE` 27→4. **Sort**: Sorts using `SortKey` (supports `BranchActivity` ordering) 28→ 29→## State Variables 30→
... [53 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: TUI Filter Toggle Pattern 3→read_when: 4→ - "adding a filter toggle to the TUI dashboard" 5→ - "implementing server-side filtering in the TUI" 6→ - "adding keybindings that toggle dashboard state" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "adding a filter toggle to the TUI dashboard" 9→ warning: "Server-side filters (like author) must clear _data_cache on toggle. Client-side filters (like stack, objective) only re-filter cached rows. Follow the 6-component pattern in filter-toggle-pattern.md." 10→--- 11→ 12→# TUI Filter Toggle Pattern 13→ 14→Pattern for adding a filter toggle to the erk TUI dashboard. The author filter is the reference implementation. 15→ 16→## 6 Required Components 17→ 18→Each component below is demonstrated by the author filter toggle. The reference files are listed in the "Reference Implementation" section at the bottom. 19→ 20→<!-- Source: src/erk/tui/app.py, _show_all_users --> 21→<!-- Source: src/erk/tui/actions/filter_actions.py, action_toggle_all_users --> 22→<!-- Source: src/erk/tui/widgets/status_bar.py, set_author_filter --> 23→<!-- Source: src/erk/tui/screens/help_screen.py, help binding labels --> 24→ 25→### 1. Field Initialization (app.py) 26→ 27→Add a boolean field (e.g., `_show_all_users = False`) in the app's `__init__`. See `ErkDashApp` in `src/erk/tui/app.py`. 28→ 29→### 2. Action Handler (filter_actions.py) 30→
... [45 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Frozen Dataclass Field Management 3→read_when: 4→ - "removing a field from a frozen dataclass" 5→ - "renaming a field in PlanRowData or similar frozen dataclass" 6→ - "getting unexpected constructor errors after field changes" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "removing a field from a frozen dataclass" 9→ warning: "Grep for the class name across ALL constructor sites. Frozen dataclasses have 5+ places to update: field definition, real provider, fake provider, test helpers, and filtering/display logic. Missing one causes runtime TypeError." 10→ - action: "using positional arguments when constructing PlanRowData" 11→ warning: "Always use keyword arguments for frozen dataclass construction. Positional arguments break silently when fields are reordered. Use make_plan_row() helper in tests." 12→--- 13→ 14→# Frozen Dataclass Field Management 15→ 16→Frozen dataclasses like `PlanRowData` have fields referenced across many files. Adding or removing a field requires updating every construction site, which is error-prone because the frozen constraint means you cannot incrementally update — all constructor calls must be correct simultaneously. 17→ 18→## The Removal Checklist 19→ 20→When removing a field from a frozen dataclass: 21→ 22→1. **Remove the field definition** from the dataclass in `data/types.py` 23→2. **Remove from real provider** — `RealPlanDataProvider._build_row_data()` in the plan data provider 24→3. **Remove from fake provider** — `FakePlanDataProvider` and the `make_plan_row()` helper in `packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/plan_data_provider/fake.py` 25→4. **Remove from display logic** — `PlanDataTable._row_to_values()` in `plan_table.py` 26→5. **Remove from any filtering/sorting logic** that references the field 27→6. **Remove from documentation** — `docs/learned/tui/plan-row-data.md` 28→ 29→The critical step is #3 — `make_plan_row()` is used extensively in tests. Missing this causes widespread test failures. 30→
... [32 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: TUI Keyboard Shortcuts Inventory 3→read_when: 4→ - "adding a new keyboard shortcut to the TUI" 5→ - "checking for shortcut conflicts before binding a new key" 6→ - "understanding what keys are available in the TUI" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "adding a new key binding without checking existing bindings" 9→ warning: "Check this document and ErkDashApp.BINDINGS in app.py for conflicts. Some keys are hidden but still active." 10→ score: 4 11→--- 12→ 13→# TUI Keyboard Shortcuts Inventory 14→ 15→Complete binding inventory from `ErkDashApp.BINDINGS` in `src/erk/tui/app.py`. 16→ 17→## All Bindings 18→ 19→<!-- Source: src/erk/tui/app.py, ErkDashApp.BINDINGS --> 20→ 21→The complete binding inventory is defined in `ErkDashApp.BINDINGS`. The bindings include both visible shortcuts (shown in help) and hidden power-user shortcuts. 22→ 23→## Key Groups 24→ 25→**Navigation:** j/k (vim-style), escape (quit) 26→ 27→**View Switching:** 1/2/3 (direct), left/right (cycle) 28→ 29→**Filters:** o (objective), t (stack), / (text filter), s (sort) 30→
... [50 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Lifecycle and PR Status Display 3→read_when: 4→ - "adding a new lifecycle stage to the TUI" 5→ - "changing lifecycle abbreviations or colors" 6→ - "modifying PR status emoji indicators" 7→ - "understanding the stage column in erk dash" 8→tripwires: 9→ - action: "adding a new lifecycle stage without updating abbreviation map" 10→ warning: "The stage column is 8 chars wide. New stages longer than 8 chars need abbreviations in compute_lifecycle_display(). Also update format_lifecycle_with_status() stage detection." 11→--- 12→ 13→# Lifecycle and PR Status Display 14→ 15→The TUI dashboard shows a `stage` column for planned_pr plans, computed in `lifecycle.py` and displayed only in planned_pr backend mode. PR status indicators (draft/published, conflicts, review decisions) are rendered inline within the stage display. 16→ 17→## Lifecycle Stage Display 18→ 19→<!-- Source: packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/plan_data_provider/lifecycle.py, compute_lifecycle_display --> 20→ 21→`compute_lifecycle_display()` reads `lifecycle_stage` from plan header fields. If absent, it infers the stage from `is_draft` and `pr_state` in plan metadata: 22→ 23→| `is_draft` | `pr_state` | Inferred Stage | 24→| ---------- | ---------- | -------------- | 25→| `True` | `"OPEN"` | `"planned"` | 26→| `False` | `"OPEN"` | `"impl"` | 27→| `False` | `"MERGED"` | `"merged"` | 28→| `False` | `"CLOSED"` | `"closed"` | 29→ 30→When the resolved stage is `"planned"` and a workflow run exists, it upgrades to `"impl"`.
... [51 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: TUI Modal Screen Pattern 3→read_when: 4→ - "adding a new modal screen to the TUI" 5→ - "implementing a ModalScreen subclass" 6→ - "displaying detail views or confirmation dialogs" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "creating a ModalScreen without CSS for dismiss behavior" 9→ warning: "ModalScreen requires explicit CSS for the overlay. Without it, clicking outside the modal does nothing." 10→ - action: "calling widget methods from @work(thread=True) without call_from_thread()" 11→ warning: "Background thread widget mutations cause silent UI corruption. Use self.app.call_from_thread(callback, ...)." 12→ - action: "using inverted key check in on_key() modal dismiss logic" 13→ warning: "if event.key not in (...) is WRONG for dismiss logic — it swallows dismiss keys. Use if event.key in (...) to check for positive dismiss. Regression caused by stacked PR merge order." 14→ score: 7 15→--- 16→ 17→# TUI Modal Screen Pattern 18→ 19→Checklist for implementing a new modal screen in the erk TUI. 20→ 21→## 7-Element Checklist 22→ 23→### 1. ModalScreen Subclass 24→ 25→Extend `ModalScreen` (not `Screen`) to get overlay dismiss behavior: 26→ 27→```python 28→from textual.screen import ModalScreen 29→ 30→class MyDetailScreen(ModalScreen):
... [101 lines omitted] ...
66→ except Exception as e: 67→ self.app.call_from_thread(self._on_error, str(e)) 80→### 6. Error Boundary 82→Wrap the entire worker body in try/except. This is an approved EAFP exception for UI error boundaries — it prevents silent worker failures. 125→- [Textual Async Best Practices](textual-async.md) — Error boundary pattern details

1→--- 2→title: Modal Widget Embedding Pattern 3→read_when: 4→ - "reusing PlanDataTable in a modal screen" 5→ - "embedding complex widgets in Textual modal screens" 6→ - "handling null safety for optional gateway fields in TUI" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "passing id= kwarg to PlanDataTable constructor" 9→ warning: "PlanDataTable does not support the id= keyword argument. Use CSS selectors or widget references instead." 10→ - action: "accessing optional gateway fields without null checks in TUI event handlers" 11→ warning: "Gateway fields like plan_body or objective_content may be None. Always check before accessing in event handlers." 12→ - action: "implementing on_key() in a modal without calling event.prevent_default() and event.stop()" 13→ warning: "Modal on_key() must call event.prevent_default() and event.stop() BEFORE any logic. Without this, keystrokes leak to the underlying view and trigger unintended actions." 14→ - action: "implementing modal dismiss with an inverted key check condition" 15→ warning: "Dismiss-on-unhandled pattern: if event.key not in (bound_keys): self.dismiss(). Using the inverted condition (if key in bound_keys: dismiss) is a common bug that dismisses on valid keys instead of unrecognized ones." 16→--- 17→ 18→# Modal Widget Embedding Pattern 19→ 20→Pattern for reusing complex widgets (like PlanDataTable) inside modal screens. Based on the ObjectivePlansScreen implementation that embeds PlanDataTable in a modal dialog. 21→ 22→## The Pattern 23→ 24→When a screen needs to display a subset of data using an existing table widget: 25→ 26→1. **Create the modal screen** extending `ModalScreen` or `Screen` 27→2. **Instantiate the widget** without custom ID (PlanDataTable limitation) 28→3. **Configure the widget** for the modal context (different columns, filtered data) 29→4. **Handle events** from the embedded widget within the modal screen 30→
... [42 lines omitted] ...

session.assistant:
Perfect! I've successfully added `curated_in: erk-tui` to the YAML frontmatter of all 10 files in `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/tui/`.
## Summary
All files have been updated with the `curated_in: erk-tui` field added as the last field before the closing `---` in their frontmatter blocks:
1. **data-contract.md** - Added after `audit_result: edited` 2. **derived-display-columns.md** - Added after the `read_when` list 3. **dual-handler-pattern.md** - Added after `audit_result: clean` 4. **filter-pipeline.md** - Added after the last tripwire entry 5. **filter-toggle-pattern.md** - Added after the tripwire entry 6. **frozen-dataclass-field-management.md** - Added after the last tripwire entry 7. **keyboard-shortcuts.md** - Added after `score: 4` 8. **lifecycle-display.md** - Added after the tripwire entry 9. **modal-screen-pattern.md** - Added after `score: 7` 10. **modal-widget-embedding.md** - Added after the last tripwire entry
Each file's frontmatter now includes the `curated_in: erk-tui` field in the correct position (immediately before the closing `---`), and no content after the frontmatter was modified.
